The Ethiopian faithful who fill the churches of Saint George on this day are not venerating a historical hero but entering into a living Paschal mystery. Amen.

24.title: St. Shenouda the Archimandrite, St. Elizabeth the Wonderworker & St. Sabas the Goth
24.narrative:
St. Shenouda the Archimandrite of White Monastery, St. Elizabeth the Wonderworker, and St. Sabas the Goth

On the twenty-fourth of Miazia, in the illuminated wake of the great feast of Saint George, the holy Church gathers three witnesses whose lives embody the three primary instruments through which the Paschal victory is communicated: the monastic community that keeps the liturgy alive, the wonder-worker through whom the Resurrection's healing power flows, and the soldier-martyr who brought the Georgian frontier into the universal Church. Saint Shenouda the Archimandrite — Shenoute of Atripe, the great White Monastery abbot — returns in the Miazia illumination with all the Paschal weight that his fierce, extraordinary life can carry. If the Lenten months portrayed Shenoute primarily as the legislator of asceticism and the defender of the poor, Miazia portrays him as the Paschal abbot: the man who presided at the great Easter vigil in a monastery of hundreds, perhaps thousands, of monks and nuns, who sang the Alleluia that ends the fast with a communal voice that the tradition imagines shaking the walls of the White Monastery. The feast of the Resurrection was, for Shenoute, the annual vindication of everything the fast had been — the proof that the emptying produced the filling, that the death produced the life, that the forty days of darkness produced the single morning of light.

Saint Elizabeth the Wonderworker is this day's discovery: a Byzantine abbess, venerated in the Eastern church as a wonder-worker of remarkable gifts, whose monastery became one of the great healing centres of Constantinople. She is occasionally confused with Elizabeth of Heraclea, but the tradition preserves her as a distinct figure whose ministry of healing encompassed not only physical ailments but the spiritual desolation that was the most common complaint of those who came to her monastery: the people who had lost their faith, or their hope, or their capacity for joy, and who found in Elizabeth's presence something that restored all three. In the Miazia illumination, Elizabeth the Wonderworker is the image of the Resurrection's most intimate application: the healing not of the body alone but of the interior life, the restoration of the person to the fullness of life that the Resurrection has made available.

Saint Sabas the Goth makes his second appearance in Miazia — already commemorated in Megabit 16 — because Basil the Great's letter in honour of his martyrdom is among the most theologically eloquent responses to the question of why a Gothic soldier drowned in a Wallachian river in three hundred and seventy-two should be commemorated by the Bishop of Caesarea and celebrated annually by the Ethiopian Church twelve centuries later. Basil's answer is the Miazia answer: that the martyrs belong to the universal Church, not to the local community that produced them, and that the blood shed in the Gothic forests is as precious to the Body of Christ as the blood shed in the arenas of Rome. Shenoute's Alleluia, Elizabeth's healing tears, Sabas's Wallachian drowning: three modes of the one Paschal victory that respects no border, speaks no single language, and is given entire to every person and every people who receive it. Amen.

25.title: St. Mark the Evangelist — The Root of the African Faith, St. Anianus of Alexandria & St. Macedonius
25.narrative:
St. Mark the Evangelist and Founder of the Alexandrian Church, St. Anianus the Second Bishop of Alexandria, and St. Macedonius the Syrian Ascetic

On the twenty-fifth of Miazia, the holy Church celebrates the feast of the holy, glorious, and all-praised Apostle and Evangelist Mark, the Companion of Peter and Paul, the author of the second Gospel, and the Founder of the Church in Alexandria and on the African continent — the feast that is, for the Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church, a feast of its own origin and identity, a celebration not merely of a past apostle but of the living root from which the Ethiopian tree of faith has grown. Saint Mark is the apostle of Africa, and his Miazia feast is the Ethiopian Church's annual act of returning to that root, of drinking again from the source from which all its tradition flows. The great Patriarch of Alexandria who today governs the Coptic church stands in an unbroken apostolic succession that runs directly back to Mark; the Ethiopian church's own patriarchal connection to Alexandria extends that same succession across the mountains and rivers of the Horn of Africa to the present day.

The tradition of Mark's arrival in Alexandria preserves a detail of extraordinary simplicity and theological richness: that when he first set foot in Alexandria and his sandal broke, he went to a cobbler to have it repaired, and that the cobbler — whose name was Anianus — accidentally drove his awl through his own hand in the process. Mark healed the wound with mud and saliva, making a small clay poultice in the gesture that recalls the Lord's healing of the man born blind in the fourth Gospel, and Anianus asked who had healed him. Mark replied with the Gospel, beginning from the prophecy of Isaiah and proceeding through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. Anianus was baptised that day, and later became the second bishop of Alexandria, the first successor of Mark. This story is the root story of African Christianity: the apostle arrives, a sandal breaks, a wound is healed, and from that small accident the greatest tradition of African Christian theology begins to grow.

The Gospel of Mark is the Paschal Gospel in its most concentrated form: shorter than Matthew, more urgently paced, less interested in discourse than in action, it moves with the momentum of a man who knows that the story he is telling is the most urgent story in the world and has no time to waste on anything that does not contribute directly to its climax. The Gospel begins with In the beginning the good news of Jesus Christ, the Son of God — a deliberate echo of Genesis, announcing that what is about to be told is a new creation — and it ends with the empty tomb and the terrified women who said nothing to anyone because they were afraid. That abrupt ending, which later scribes tried to smooth over with additional verses, is in the Ethiopian reading the most powerful ending in the entire canon: the story that cannot be ended because it is not over, the empty tomb that remains open into the present day, the silence of the women that is broken by every Christian who tells the story they were too frightened to tell. Mark wrote the Gospel of the Resurrection and did not conclude it, because the Resurrection does not conclude.

Saint Macedonius the Syrian Ascetic brings this great feast to its close with the figure of the contemplative who received Mark's Gospel in the tradition of Syrian monasticism and who transmitted it through the form of his own life. A monk of the Syrian region who practised a particularly demanding form of asceticism — living for years in an enclosure so small that he could neither stand nor lie down — Macedonius was known for a gift of prophecy and a particular tenderness toward those who had fallen from their early fervour. He is the representative in this assembly of the vast tradition of Syrian monasticism that received the African Gospel of Mark through the Antiochene church and transformed it into the distinctive forms of Syrian spiritual life: the Stylites and the enclosure-dwellers, the wandering monks and the anchorites, the Syrian poets and the Syrian doctors, all drawing from the same Marcan root that was planted in the sand of Alexandria when a cobbler's hand was healed by an apostle's clay. Amen.

26.title: St. Susenyos the Martyr, St. Victor, St. Basil of Amasea & St. Glaphyra
26.narrative:
St. Susenyos the Martyr of Ethiopia, St. Victor the Martyr, St. Basil of Amasea, and St. Glaphyra the Handmaid

On the twenty-sixth of Miazia, the holy Church gathers four witnesses whose lives together illuminate the social sweep of the Paschal victory — from the king of a Christian realm to the handmaid of a royal household, from a bishop of the Pontic coast to a martyr of the African frontier. Saint Susenyos is commemorated in the Ethiopian tradition as a martyr and royal figure — a name that appears in the Ethiopian saints' tradition associated with the defence of Christological orthodoxy and the personal cost of royal piety. His commemoration in Miazia 26, two days after the feast of Mark and two days before the monthly commemoration of the Crucifixion, places him in the specifically Paschal register: the Christian king who dies for his faith is the political dimension of the Paschal mystery, the application of the Resurrection's victory to the question of how power should be exercised and for what it should be sacrificed.

Saint Victor the Martyr returns in his monthly aspect — the Victor, the one who has conquered — on the twenty-sixth day with the full weight of the Miazia illumination behind his name. In the second half of this month, the name Victor has ceased to be simply a name and has become a theological title: the entire community of Miazia's commemorations is a community of victors, of those whose apparent defeat — by the sword, by exile, by the slow erosion of imprisonment — has been revealed by the Resurrection as the deepest form of victory. Victor's name is the month's name, and his presence on this twenty-sixth day is the tradition's way of saying that the victory is nearly complete, that the month's journey is approaching its harvest.

Saint Basil of Amasea is this day's discovery of pastoral episcopal courage in the most socially uncomfortable form: a bishop of Amasea in Pontus who was martyred under the Emperor Licinius for a specific pastoral intervention. Basil had given shelter in his episcopal household to a young Christian noblewoman named Glaphyra who had fled the court of Licinius's son-in-law Constans, who was pursuing her against her will. Licinius demanded Glaphyra's return; Basil refused; Licinius had Basil executed. The bishop died because he had given shelter to the fugitive, and the tradition honours him as a martyr of the specific form of pastoral charity that the Law and the Prophets call the defence of the widow, the orphan, and the fugitive — the ministry of asylum that the bishop's house has exercised since the earliest centuries of the Church's life.

Saint Glaphyra is the fourth witness of this day and the one whose presence makes the entire assembly fully clear: she survived Basil's execution, lived in the episcopal household under his successor's protection, and eventually died in peace, having spent years in the contemplative life that her flight from the imperial court had, paradoxically, made possible. She is the fugitive who found in asylum what she had been seeking, the handmaid whose freedom was purchased by a bishop's blood. In the Miazia illumination, Glaphyra's survival is the point: the bishop who died to protect her was not defeated; the woman who was protected lived on in the liberty his death had secured. This is the logic of the Paschal mystery — the one who gives his life does not lose it, and the one for whose sake the gift is given carries the giver's life forward in her own. Amen.

27.title: The Monthly Crucifixion, St. Simeon of Jerusalem & St. Stephen of Taron
27.narrative:
The Monthly Commemoration of the Crucifixion, St. Simeon the Second Bishop of Jerusalem, and St. Stephen of Taron

On the twenty-seventh of Miazia, the monthly commemoration of the Crucifixion falls in the full blaze of the Paschal illumination, and the juxtaposition is the tradition's most searching theological act: what does the Cross mean when it is commemorated not in the darkness of Lent but in the light of the month that follows the Resurrection? The monthly return to the Crucifixion in Miazia forces the community to hold together two realities that the liturgical calendar otherwise separates: the death and the life, the suffering and the glory, the Cross and the empty tomb. In the Ethiopian tradition, these two are never fully separated even in ordinary time — the cross that hangs in every Ethiopian church, the cross that the faithful wear, is always the sign of both the death and the life, the instrument of execution that has become the throne of glory. But in Miazia, with the Paschal feast so recently completed or so nearly approaching, the monthly Crucifixion is received with a specific awareness: that the death we commemorate is the death that has already been answered, that the Cross we venerate is the Cross of the risen Christ, that the wound in his side is the wound that Thomas was invited to touch and that remains open not as a scar of defeat but as a window into the heart of God.

Saint Simeon the Second Bishop of Jerusalem is the figure of apostolic continuity through suffering: the son of Clopas, the kinsman of the Lord, who governed the Jerusalem church after the martyrdom of James the Lord's brother and who himself was martyred under Trajan at the extraordinary age of one hundred and twenty years, having survived the destruction of the city, the reconstitution of the community, the Domitian persecution, and the full arc of the apostolic and post-apostolic age. He was the last bishop of Jerusalem who could claim a direct kinship with Christ Himself, and his martyrdom closed a chapter of the Church's history that could not be reopened. In the Miazia Crucifixion commemoration, Simeon is the figure who stands at the end of the apostolic era and looks back across it toward the Cross that had founded everything, which he had known through those who had stood at its foot.

Saint Stephen of Taron is this day's discovery from the Armenian church: a bishop and historian of the Armenian Christian tradition whose theological and historical writings preserved the memory of the Armenian martyrs and confessors against the Persian and later Byzantine pressures that threatened to erase it. His work is the Armenian version of what Eusebius did for the universal church and what Marouta did for the Persian church — the act of keeping the record, of insisting that the blood shed for the faith in a particular corner of the world will not be forgotten, of placing the local martyrdom in the context of the universal witness. The monthly Crucifixion, Simeon who survived to the end of the apostolic age, Stephen who kept the Armenian memory: together they are the tradition's image of the Cross's universal scope — reaching every age, every nation, every generation. Amen.

28.title: St. Melius the Martyr, St. Jason, St. Sosipater & The Apostles of the Seventy
28.narrative:
St. Melius the Martyr, St. Jason of Tarsus the Apostle, St. Sosipater of Iconium the Apostle, and the Companions of the Seventy

On the twenty-eighth of Miazia, the holy Church gathers the Apostles Jason and Sosipater alongside their companion Melius the Martyr, and in their assembly the apostolic mission in its most extended form — the mission that stretched from Paul's companions outward to the ends of the earth — is placed at the threshold of the month's great closing feasts. Saint Melius the Martyr is a confessor of the North African or Eastern church whose commemoration on this day of apostolic companionship establishes the link between the apostolic mission and the martyrological testimony: the apostles founded the communities; the martyrs sealed them. The Church that was planted by the Seventy and the companions of Paul was the Church that produced the martyrs who gave it its enduring vitality, and Melius represents the second and third generation of that planted faith — no longer the ones who heard the apostles directly but the ones who received from those who heard, and who gave their lives with the same readiness.

Saint Jason of Tarsus is one of the most historically attested of the Seventy: greeted by Paul in the Letter to the Romans, his house was the shelter that Paul and Silas occupied during the Thessalonican mission that ended with the riot described in Acts 17, and the mob that sought Paul went first to Jason's house, arrested Jason and his companions, and dragged them before the city authorities, charging them with harbouring those who had turned the world upside down. That charge — turning the world upside down — is one of the most accurate descriptions of the apostolic mission in the entire New Testament, and Jason accepted it as such, paying the bond required for their release and continuing his apostolic work. Tradition sends him eventually to the island of Corfu, where he became the first bishop and where his relics are still venerated.

Saint Sosipater of Iconium is Jason's companion in the Romans 16 greeting and his partner in the Corfu apostolate: a man of Iconium who had been drawn into the apostolic circle during Paul's mission to that city and who accompanied Jason in the westward mission to the Greek islands. Their partnership is the tradition's image of the collaborative character of the apostolic mission — not solitary heroes but pairs and teams, sent out two by two as the Lord had sent the Seventy, sustaining each other through the hardships of the long missionary journey and confirming each other's witness before the communities they planted. The Ethiopian Church honours Jason and Sosipater together as a pair because their witness is, in the deepest sense, inseparable: one without the other would have accomplished less, and the Church their partnership founded is the testimony of what two faithful people can do when they go forward together in the name of the Lord.

The Companions of the Seventy are the unnamed remainder of the apostolic company that Jason and Sosipater represent: those whose names appear in the Romans 16 greetings alongside them, those whose names have been preserved in regional traditions but lost to the universal church, and those whose names are known only to God. They are gathered on this twenty-eighth day of Miazia as the tradition's final insistence that the apostolic mission was a movement of communities rather than of heroic individuals, and that the Church that approaches the great closing feasts of this month does so on the foundation of their collective, often anonymous, always costly witness. Amen.

29.title: The Triple Feast — Monthly, St. Memnon the Wonderworker & St. Dadas of Persia
29.narrative:
The Monthly Feast of the Annunciation, Nativity and Resurrection; St. Memnon the Wonderworker; and St. Dadas of Persia

On the twenty-ninth of Miazia, the holy Church celebrates the monthly Triple Feast — the Annunciation, the Nativity, and the Resurrection — in the most charged position it occupies in the entire calendar year: the penultimate day of the month of the Paschal dawn, in the immediate aftermath of the great feast of Saint George and on the threshold of the final double commemoration that will close the cycle. The Triple Feast in the month of the Resurrection is not a commemoration looking backward at events long past but a proclamation of what the community has been living through all month: the Annunciation whose mystery was deepened by the Tinsae Maryam of Megabit 29, the Nativity whose joy was prepared by the Lenten darkness, and the Resurrection that is the meaning and completion of both. To celebrate all three on this twenty-ninth day of Miazia is to say that the entire arc of the Incarnation — from the angel's word in Nazareth to the empty tomb in the garden — is a single indivisible act of divine love, and that the month of Miazia has been the living of that arc in compressed liturgical time.

Saint Memnon the Wonderworker is this day's discovery of a figure who stands at the intersection of the apostolic tradition and the age of the Councils: a bishop of Ephesus at the time of the Council of Ephesus in four hundred and thirty-one, whose passionate advocacy for the definition of Mary as Theotokos was a pastoral and theological act of the first importance. Memnon had governed the Ephesian community for years before Cyril of Alexandria arrived for the council, and he brought to the Cyrillian cause not only theological agreement but local authority — the community of Mary's own city, the community that tradition said had received the Theotokos into its midst at the end of her earthly life, standing in support of her title. His wonder-working gifts, reported throughout his episcopal ministry, are in the Miazia illumination the practical expression of the same faith that produced his conciliar witness: the bishop who believes that Mary's Son is fully God and fully human is also the bishop whose prayer for the sick is answered.

Saint Dadas of Persia is among the most affecting of the Persian martyrs: an officer of the Persian court who was converted to Christianity through witnessing the torture and death of Christian confessors, and who was subsequently arrested alongside members of his family and household. The tradition records that Dadas faced his execution with such complete interior stillness that the soldiers around him were disturbed by it, unable to understand how a man who had held high position at the Persian court could face the loss of everything — rank, wealth, family, life — with the equanimity of one who has exchanged it all for something more valuable than the sum of everything surrendered. In the Miazia illumination, Dadas is the Persian version of the Triple Feast's meaning: the one who received the Incarnation's gift in the form of a converted heart, celebrated its Nativity in the new life of faith, and entered into its Resurrection in the martyrdom that completed his conversion. The feast of feasts, Memnon at Ephesus, Dadas in Persia: the Paschal mystery given entire to every person, every place, every language. Amen.

30.title: St. Mark the Evangelist — Monthly, St. James the Brother of the Lord & St. Clement of Rome
30.narrative:
St. Mark the Evangelist, St. James the Brother of the Lord First Bishop of Jerusalem, and St. Clement of Rome

On the thirtieth and final day of Miazia, the holy Church closes the month of the Paschal dawn with an assembly that constitutes the most deliberately constructed conclusion in the entire Synaxarium cycle: the Evangelist who founded the African church, the brother of the Lord who governed the mother church in Jerusalem, and the third successor of Peter in Rome — the three great pillars of the apostolic era standing together at the close of the month of the Resurrection to declare that the victory whose approach has been Miazia's theme has been won, is being celebrated, and will be transmitted across every age to come. The monthly commemoration of Saint Mark on the thirtieth day returns the community to the feast that was celebrated on the twenty-fifth, but now in the specific register of the month's closing: Mark as the guardian of the tradition, the one who ensures that what was received is transmitted, the evangelist whose written word carries the Paschal proclamation forward through time after the community of eyewitnesses has gone.

Saint James the Brother of the Lord stands in this assembly as the figure of the first Christian generation's most challenging vocation: to have known Jesus of Nazareth in the flesh, to have lived in the same household, to have watched him grow up, and then to have come, through the experience of the Resurrection, to the recognition that the one you had known as your brother was the Lord of Heaven and Earth. James's conversion — which tradition places after the Resurrection, when the risen Christ appeared to him — was the most personally intimate of all the post-Resurrection encounters: not the ecstasy of Mary Magdalene in the garden, not the trembling of the disciples in the locked room, but the meeting of a brother who had doubted with a Brother whose death he had perhaps witnessed and whose resurrection he was now confronted with face to face. James subsequently became the pillar of the Jerusalem church, governing it for three decades with a strictness of personal asceticism and a breadth of pastoral charity that made him revered by Jewish and Christian communities alike, until he was thrown from the pinnacle of the Temple and stoned in the year sixty-two.

Saint Clement of Rome is the final witness of this month and one of the most significant in the entire apostolic inheritance: the third or fourth successor of Peter in the Roman see (the order of his predecessors is debated), whose letter to the Corinthian community — the earliest surviving Christian document outside the New Testament — demonstrates that the first-century Roman church understood itself to have a responsibility for the welfare and order of communities it had not founded and across which it had no simple jurisdictional authority. Clement wrote to Corinth to address a situation of disorder, not with the authority of a superior but with the pastoral concern of an elder for a community in trouble, citing the example of the martyrs, the theology of the Old Testament, and the witness of the Resurrection as the grounds for the unity and order he was urging. His letter ends with one of the earliest extended liturgical prayers in the Christian tradition, a prayer of extraordinary beauty that reaches across the centuries to connect the Clement who prayed it with every community that has prayed the same prayer in the same Spirit ever since.

Miazia closes with Mark who planted the African church, James who governed the mother church, and Clement who wrote to a troubled community from Rome: three figures of the apostolic tradition's most essential work, which is not the dramatic gesture but the patient transmission of the living faith from one generation to the next. The month of the Paschal dawn ends not with the silence of the empty tomb — which was the ending of Mark's Gospel — but with the voice of the living community praying across the centuries, the voice that Mark heard at the foot of Peter's chair, that James heard in the Jerusalem upper room, that Clement wrote into his letter to Corinth, and that the Ethiopian Church has been singing since its own apostolic dawn. Miazia is complete; the Paschal cycle is accomplished; the Resurrection has been proclaimed in every tongue and witnessed in every land. Christ is risen. Amen.
